statement.en: Who Defines "Best"? Towards Interactive, User-Defined Evaluation of LLM Leaderboards
statement.summary: LLM leaderboards are widely used to compare models and guide deployment decisions. However, leaderboard rankings are shaped by evaluation priorities set by benchmark designers, rather than by the diverse goals and constraints of actual users and organizations. A single aggregate score often obscures how models behave across different prompt types and compositions. In this work, we conduct an in-depth analysis of the dataset used in the LMArena (formerly Chatbot Arena) benchmark and investigate this evaluation challenge by designing an interactive visualization interface as a design probe. Our analysis reveals that the dataset is heavily skewed toward certain topics, that model rankings vary across prompt slices, and that preference-based judgments are used in ways that blur their intended

proposer: Minji Jung, Minjae Lee, Yejin Kim et al.

reiTyping.dfumt8Reason: The axiom presents a provisional empirical and methodological claim (2026 preprint). The core assertion—that leaderboard rankings reflect designer priorities rather than user goals, and that interactive customization improves evaluation—is supported by preliminary evidence (Arena dataset analysis, interface design probe) but awaits peer validation, replication across other benchmarks, and longitudinal adoption metrics. The work is not yet established consensus but is a defensible research direction under active development.
